mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW M6 engine typically costs £5,000–£14,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the S38 at £5,000–£8,000+ to the S85 at £8,000–£14,000+ and the S63 at £7,000–£12,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M6 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S38B35 in the E24 — one of BMW's most durable engines, 200k+ miles is routine with proper maintenance. The S63B44 in the F06/F12/F13 is proven and robust. The S85B50 in the E63/E64 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing, SMG pump, and throttle actuator caveats.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M6 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but an S85 (E63/E64) without documented rod bearing replacement, SMG pump history, or throttle actuator replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is a masterpiece; the known risks are the problem. An F13 S63 has no such caveats. The E24 S38 is bulletproof.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my M6 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: rod bearing knock (S85), metallic noise at high RPM (S85), oil drips (S63), SMG pump failure (E63), throttle actuator failure (S85). On the S85, cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is a warning. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £5,000–£14,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M6 engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on generation. Against a £40,000 F13, a £7,000–£12,000+ S63 recon is 18–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E63, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 40–70% — ⚠️ watch. Against an E24, engine work is restoration — the values justify the investment. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M6 should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: F13 M6 Competition with the S63 — 600 hp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£45,000). For the classic, E24 M635CSi with the S38 — the original M grand tourer, bulletproof engine, and a genuine investment. For the V10 experience, E63/E64 with the S85 — the only V10 M6, but budget for rod bearings (£3,000–£5,000) and SMG pump (£2,500–£5,000) before you buy.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E24, E63/E64, F06/F12/F13) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW M6 vs M5 — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they share engines — the S85 in the E60 M5/E63 M6 and the S63 in the F10/F90 M5/F06/F12/F13 M6 are identical. The M6 is a grand tourer; the M5 is a super saloon. Reliability is identical; the M6 is slightly heavier but works the powertrain harder. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M5 offers more practicality; the M6 offers more style and presence.

hero.h1: M6 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW M6 engine across three generations — from the M88/S38 straight-six of the E24 M635CSi to the S85 V10 of the E63/E64 and the S63 twin-turbo V8 of the F06/F12/F13. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's original grand tourer. Backed by real UK M6 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The M6 rule — grand tourer, grand bills: an S63 recon at £7,000–£12,000+ against a £40,000 F13 is 18–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E63, an S85 recon at £8,000–£14,000+ is 40–70% — ⚠️ watch. Generation decides everything. The E24 is now a classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.
★★★★☆ — the S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S63 twin-turbo V8 is proven and robust; the S38 straight-six is bulletproof

S85B50 (E63/E64) — rod bearing failure, SMG pump failure, throttle actuator issues
F13 M6 Competition with S63 — 600 hp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory

👑 The Original Grand Tourer — BMW's most luxurious performance coupe before the 8 Series

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The M6 is BMW's grand touring icon — three generations of V10 howl, twin-turbo V8 muscle, and straight-six purity. The S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S63 is proven and robust; the S38 is bulletproof. Buy the newest S63 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained E24 classic — and on any E63 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy.

H2: BMW M6 Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Three generations, 35 years, and one nameplate that defined the grand touring coupe. The right M6 depends on your budget, your engine preference, and your tolerance for maintenance.

rows.value: Our Verdict: The original M6 — a hand-built grand touring coupe with the M88 engine from the M1 supercar. The S38 straight-six is one of BMW's most durable engines, revving to 7,000 rpm with individual throttle bodies. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The E24 M6 is the ultimate M6 investment.

rows.metric: BMW M6 E63/E64 🔥 Most Enquired — S85 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The V10 M6 — a masterpiece that shared its engine with the E60 M5. The S85 revs to 8,250 rpm with a sound that few engines can match. But the rod bearing failure is the defining risk: budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement. The SMG III gearbox is also a weak point. A masterpiece — but a costly one.

rows.metric: BMW M6 F06/F12/F13 🔥 Most Enquired — S63 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The third-generation M6 — available as a Coupé (F13), Convertible (F12), and for the first time, a four-door Gran Coupé (F06). The S63 twin-turbo V8 produces 560 hp (standard) to 600 hp (Competition). The engine is proven and robust. The F13 M6 is the M6 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "M6" Means Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "M6" badge covers three different engines across three generations — the M88/S38 straight-six (E24), S85 V10 (E63/E64), and S63 twin-turbo V8 (F06/F12/F13). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile. The engine family — not the badge — determines ownership experience and costs.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M6 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW M6 — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: S63B44 and S85B50 ratings: 90% confidence — well-documented across the M range with clear patterns from specialist rebuilders. S85 rod bearing and SMG pump failures are extensively documented. S63 is proven and robust. E24 engines: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the classic M6 era, but not captured in proprietary volume data. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The S85 V10 (E63/E64) rod bearing failure is the defining issue of the generation — bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Preventative replacement is recommended at 60,000–80,000 miles. Correction applied: The S85 V10 produces 507 bhp at 7,750 rpm and 384 lb-ft of torque at 6,100 rpm, with an 8,250 rpm redline. This engine was inspired by BMW's Formula 1 program. Correction applied: The S63 (F06/F12/F13) produces 560 hp in standard form and up to 600 hp in Competition Package form. The engine shares its architecture with the F10 M5. Correction applied: The E24 M6/M635CSi used two engines — the M88/3 (early cars) and the S38B35 (later cars). Both are 3.5L straight-six engines producing 282–286 bhp. The S38 is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine with proper maintenance. Correction applied: The E24 M6 is the original M6 — known as the M635CSi in European markets. The car started the tradition of giving the sportiest version of each model range the M denomination.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the M6 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining E63 M6 issue — rod bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is the warning. Affects E63/E64 M6 (2005–2010). £8,000–£14,000+ recon. Preventative replacement: £3,000–£5,000.
The SMG III automated manual gearbox pump fails, leaving the car stuck in gear. Affects E63/E64 M6 (2005–2010). £2,500–£5,000 repair. Many owners convert to manual.
The S85's throttle actuators are a known weak point — failure causes loss of power, limp mode, and check engine light. Affects E63/E64 M6. £1,500–£3,000 repair.
The S63 twin-turbo V8 suffers from oil leaks — valve cover gaskets, oil filter housing, and turbo oil lines. Affects F06/F12/F13 M6. £1,500–£3,000 repair if caught early; engine damage if ignored.
The S63's turbochargers can fail due to oil starvation or heat soak; cooling system components can also fail. Affects F06/F12/F13 M6. Turbo replacement: £3,000–£6,000.

The E24 M6's S38 is one of BMW's most durable engines — no catastrophic failure modes. Just standard maintenance: cooling system, suspension, and rust prevention. The E24 is the M6 to buy for worry-free classic ownership.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW M6 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the F13 is a modern classic [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — the V10 M6 is appreciating [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
➡ Stable — classic market, not failure-driven [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The M6 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S85 rod bearings, S63 oil leaks) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M6 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The F13 M6 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 600 hp, the S63 engine, proven and robust, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£45,000). The S63 is well-understood; the Competition package adds significant performance. The F13 is the M6 for buyers who want the ultimate modern grand tourer. (Editorial synthesis.)

The E63/E64 M6 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating classic — 8,250 rpm, 507 bhp, and the only V10 M6 ever made. Rod bearings are the defining risk — budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement or buy one that's already been done. The SMG III pump is the other caveat — budget £2,500–£5,000 for that too. The E63 is a masterpiece — but a costly one. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M6 Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one M6 based on this page, buy an F13 M6 Competition with the S63 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 600 hp, proven twin-turbo V8, and depreciated into sensible territory (£30,000–£45,000). If your budget stretches, the E24 M635CSi with the S38 is the ultimate classic — the original M grand tourer and a genuine investment. And on an E63 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy — it's £3,000–£5,000 well spent.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW M6 Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW M6 engine typically takes 18–26 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The M6 rule — grand tourer, grand bills: An £7,000–£12,000+ S63 recon against a £40,000 F13 sits at 18–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E63, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 40–70% — ⚠️ watch. Against an E24, engine work is restoration — the values justify the investment. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Factor in preventative work (S85 rod bearings: £3,000–£5,000; S63 oil leaks: £1,500–£3,000) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any M6.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW M6 Engine Range Evolved — 1983 to 2019

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW created the M6 by fitting the M1's engine into the E24 6 Series. The M88/3 was a 3.5L straight-six derived from the M1 supercar, producing 282–286 bhp.
eras.worthKnowing: The E24 M6 was hand-built — the ultimate grand tourer of its era. The S38 is one of BMW's most durable engines — 200k+ miles is routine. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's only V10 M engine — inspired by Formula One, revving to 8,250 rpm and producing 507 bhp. The SMG III gearbox was a new innovation. The E64 was the first M6 Convertible.
eras.worthKnowing: The S85 is a masterpiece — but rod bearings, SMG pumps, and throttle actuators are the defining risks. Budget £3,000–£5,000 for rod bearings before you buy.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The third-generation M6 arrived with the S63 twin-turbo V8 — 560–600 hp. The Gran Coupé (F06) was a new four-door body style, joining the Coupé (F13) and Convertible (F12).
eras.worthKnowing: The S63 is proven and robust — the F13 M6 Competition with 600 hp is the pick. The Gran Coupé is the most practical M6. After 2018, the M6 was discontinued — replaced by the M8.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW M6?

profiles.verdict: The M6 is built for this — the F13 M6 with the S63 delivers 600 hp, wafting comfort, and the perfect balance of luxury and performance. The S63 is proven and robust; the chassis is magnificent. The F13 Competition is the ultimate M6 grand tourer.

The M6 is a GT, not a track car — but 600 hp and a twin-turbo V8 means it's devastatingly fast. The F13 is the pick; the E63 with the S85 V10 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing and SMG pump risks.

The E24 M6 is a genuine classic — the original M grand tourer and a six-figure investment. The E63 M6 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating masterpiece — the only V10 M6. The F13 M6 Competition is the modern keeper — the last V8 M6 before the M8.

A £15,000 E63 M6 is not a cheap M6 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £30,000 F13 M6 Competition is the bargain; a £15,000 E63 without rod bearings done is the trap.
The F06 M6 Gran Coupé is one of the most beautiful BMWs ever made — four-door coupe styling, frameless doors, and a sloping roofline. The F12 Convertible is the ultimate open-top grand tourer.
